data.title: AI has sounded the alarm for cryptocurrency security.
data.description:
Author: Haotian; Source: X, @tmel0211

Many people probably didn't expect this, did they? Before the quantum threat even arrived, AI had already sounded the alarm for Crypto security.

What is EVMbench? Don't be confused by the name; it is essentially a practical test suite specifically designed to measure the capabilities of AI agents in smart contract security.

The test suite includes 120 high-risk vulnerabilities from auditing competitions such as Code4rena, and also includes an auditing scenario specifically designed for Tempo's new stablecoin L1.

So how exactly does the AI ​​perform this? OpenAI didn't just have it perform simple static code scanning; instead, it put it through real-world attack and defense drills in a sandbox environment. The assessment focused on three dimensions: Detect, Patch, and Exploit—essentially handing over the entire auditing work to the AI. However, what's truly unsettling is the astonishing speed at which AI has evolved in its ability to "attack and cause damage." Six months ago, GPT-5 had a success rate of only 31.9% in attack mode, while GPT-5.3-Codex has now soared to 72.2%. This means that in the near future, the hunters deep within the dark forest of smart contracts may no longer be genius hackers, but rather tireless, self-evolving AI agents operating 24/7. Many people are curious why it's easier to cause damage than to fix code. This is actually the underlying logic of the security community: White hat hackers need to understand complex business intent to plug all vulnerabilities, while hackers only need to find a crack to launch a precise attack. Since the "spear" has become so sharp, how should the "shield" evolve? I think this is the fundamental reason why Paradigm partnered with OpenAI to launch EVMbench. Since AI's attack capabilities have become so powerful, leading VCs in the Crypto industry must plan ahead and strengthen their defensive AI capabilities. As for OpenAI taking the lead in this endeavor, besides showcasing its AI capabilities, it seems more about defining security standards and then rapidly expanding its territory in the vast Crypto application market. Could it be that security auditing firms, with years of experience in the industry, are ultimately going to be defeated by OpenAI?

data.title: The Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) outlines the key priorities for cryptocurrency policy in 2026.
data.description:
Authors:Paul S. Atkins, Chairman of the SEC & Hester M. Peirce, Commissioner of the SEC;Translation: Aki, Wu Blockchain

This article is a transcript of a conversation between U.S. SEC Chairman Atkins and Hester M. Peirce on February 18, 2026, at ETHDenver.

Peirce: It is an honor to share the stage with Chairman Atkins today. Before we begin, I would like to remind everyone that our statements are personal statements made within the scope of our respective official duties and do not necessarily represent the views of the Commission or other commissioners. Chairman Atkins needs little introduction, but I will still briefly provide some background information.

Mr. Atkins was sworn in as the 34th Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on April 21st of last year. Prior to returning to the SEC, Chairman Atkins most recently served as CEO of Patomak Global Partners, a consulting firm he founded in 2009. Chairman Atkins served as an SEC commissioner from 2002 to 2008, during which time he championed regulatory transparency and consistency and promoted the use of cost-benefit analysis in institutional work. Mr. Atkins began his career as a lawyer in New York, primarily handling various corporate transactions for U.S. and foreign clients, including public and private offerings and mergers and acquisitions. He spent two and a half years based in his firm's Paris office and obtained the French "conseil juridique" (legal counsel) qualification. He is a member of the New York and Florida Bar Associations, holds a Juris Doctor (J.D.) from Vanderbilt University School of Law, and an A.B. (Phi Beta Kappa) from Wofford College (1980). President Atkins was born in Lillington, North Carolina, and grew up in Tampa, Florida. He and his wife Sarah have three sons. Interestingly, President Atkins is fluent in German and French. He's probably considering adding another language to his repertoire. Mr. President, have you considered learning Solidity? Atkins: No need. Vibe coding is perfectly adequate. Compared to BASIC-PLUS and COBOL I used in college, this is a huge improvement. Peirce: That makes sense, Mr. Chairman. But if your AI-written smart contracts start claiming "everything is a security," then we have to question whether it's an AI delusion. A few years ago, if someone had told me I would be standing on a crypto conference stage with the SEC Chairman, I would have thought they were talking nonsense. But we are indeed standing here—so let's get down to business. Over the past year, with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), under the leadership of Chairman Atkins, and during the period at the beginning of the year led by Acting Chairman Uyeda, we have taken many steps to “clarify” crypto regulation, including: Proactively soliciting and receiving written responses to a set of challenging questions covering a wide range of crypto topics; Holding numerous in-depth roundtables on several specific topics, including: the definition, trading, custody, tokenization, DeFi, and privacy; and “Crypto Go Global” initiatives in Washington, D.C., both online and offline, and across the country. During the exchange activities, numerous developers and builders were met; technical assistance was provided to the United States Congress in advancing crypto legislation; a new cooperation initiative was launched with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to establish a long-term foundation for regulatory coordination and cooperation in areas of common concern, including crypto; the practice of "regulation by enforcement" was ended; multiple staff guidance documents and FAQs were released to help the market understand what SEC staff consider to be within and outside the SEC's jurisdiction (covering issues such as mining, staking, memes, stablecoins, etc.), and how regulated entities should follow existing rules when engaging in crypto-related businesses; some unhelpful staff guidance documents, such as SAB 121, were repealed; Released a staff statement concerning broker-dealer custody of “crypto asset securities”; released a cross-departmental staff statement proposing a taxonomy for tokenized securities; approved generic listing standards for crypto ETPs (Exchange Traded Products); issued staff “no-action letters” to several projects, including tokenization and DePIN-related projects; and initiated a process for rule design, exemption relief, and committee interpretation to lay the foundation for a sustainable and stable regulatory framework. Mr. Chairman, could you please give us a preview of what developments we can expect in crypto regulation this year? Atkins: We have a lot of work to move forward with. In addition to continuing to communicate on the important legislative work that Congress is undertaking, as you mentioned, we are also advancing regulatory efforts through “Project Crypto.” This project is now being carried out as a joint initiative with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). As you all know, one of our own people—Mike Selig—was previously brought in by Commissioner Hester M. Peirce to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) as the lead legal counsel for the Crypto Task Force in my office; he is now the Chairman of the CFTC. We plan to jointly advance a series of important initiatives—regulatory coordination and joint rulemaking—to create an unprecedented joint and collaborative regulatory approach, especially given the past clashes between the two agencies on regulatory boundaries. Regarding the SEC, I expect the Commission and its staff to focus on the following in the coming weeks and months: A framework document at the Commission level: Explaining how we view crypto assets that may constitute “investment contracts” and are therefore subject to regulation. How are investment contracts formed? And how are they terminated? Innovation Exemption: Allows limited trading of certain tokenized securities on new platforms to explore and gradually develop a long-term regulatory framework in practice. Rulemaking Proposal: Establishes a more common-sense and workable path for market participants to raise funds in scenarios related to the sale of crypto assets. Letters of No Action and Exemption Orders: Provides further clarification, including responses to questions regarding whether wallets and other user interfaces (UIs) need to be registered under the Securities Exchange Act; clarification will be provided for those not subject to registration. **Brokerage/Dealer Custody Rulemaking:** Develop rules for brokerage/dealer custody of “non-security crypto assets,” including payment stablecoins. **Search Agent Modernization Rulemaking:** Promote updates to the transfer agent system to accommodate the potential role of blockchain in record keeping. **Supplementary Guidance and No-Action Letters:** Continue to provide additional guidance and no-action letters to help market participants understand how existing rules apply in their specific contexts.

Peirce: It sounds like a lot of work, but for us "securities rules enthusiasts," this experience is a bit like participating in the Olympics—the excitement is almost no less than skiing down a slope at 80 mph, performing a high-difficulty maneuver in mid-air, or doing a quadruple jump followed by a backflip on ice. While we are far less "dramatic" than Olympic champions, we do have a rare opportunity to re-examine a large number of complex regulatory issues in the context of this new technology. This task also requires "aerial skills," and we don't want to hurt or break anything—the only thing to break are unnecessary regulatory barriers that hinder technological progress.

I'd like to take a moment to talk about "innovation exemptions." The expectations and concerns they've raised may need to be tempered.

I'd like to take a moment to talk about "innovation exemptions." The expectations and concerns they've raised may need to be tempered.

I'd like to take a moment to talk about "innovation exemptions." In fact, the way people talk about it now reminds me of those who buy abandoned lockers: they're convinced there must be a rare masterpiece and a box full of gold bars inside. Similarly, some are convinced the innovation exemption will solve all their regulatory pain points at once. On the other hand, some in traditional finance (TradFi) seem to think this soon-to-be-opened locker holds a monster—one that will devour the entire traditional financial system in an ugly way. They worry the innovation exemption will allow crypto companies to ignore all rules. Both sides will likely eventually find that the innovation exemption isn't as "disruptive" as either side imagines. It will be an important step in making tokenized securities more readily integrated into the existing financial system, but it won't change the entire financial system overnight. What we're doing now is still incremental progress—as always. The goal is to facilitate the absorption of new technologies into the system in a "naturally growing" manner: enhancing the system's vitality and resilience while enabling it to more effectively serve investors, businesses, and other users of capital. Paul, could you elaborate on what your envisioned innovation exemption would look like? Atkins: I'm inclined to consider establishing an "innovation exemption" that allows both traditional financial players and crypto-native institutions to experiment within certain boundaries. For example, allowing market participants to trade certain tokenized securities through Automated Market Makers (AMMs), even if the mechanism may not be "controlled" by any single person or group. In my view, market participants should be able to interact with decentralized applications on a public, permissionless blockchain, provided they are willing. However, I also anticipate that many Americans will prefer intermediaries to hold their assets and trade on their behalf. The choice of intermediaries should be made by individual investors, not by the SEC. I also hope to discuss whether a "safe harbor" should be provided for participants who may effectively facilitate such transactions. Specifically, I would like to explore how issuers intending to tokenize their securities can collaborate with transfer agents or other tokenization agents to tokenize the securities, enabling them to be traded on-chain through AMMs or other trading systems, environments, or platforms that provide decentralized liquidity. Following this potential path, the innovation exemption would cap the size (volume) of transactions and potentially grant exemptions to certain rules and other requirements—requirements that may not be relevant under this technology. Buyers and sellers of tokenized securities would need to go through a whitelisting process. This exemption would be temporary, but long enough to allow us to assess whether new rules or revisions to existing rules are needed to allow such transactions to continue under appropriate conditions and to enable any relevant parties requiring registration to do so. I welcome feedback on this potential solution. Peirce: Thank you for giving us a glimpse of the lockers. There was no Picasso, but no terrifying monsters either. It was just a gradual step from which market participants could learn and potentially help us move towards a "fit-for-purpose," long-term sustainable regulatory framework. Speaking of new things, you and I have both seen some demonstrations showing us how these technologies (such as decentralized exchanges) work. What impressed you most about what you saw? Atkins: One interesting aspect of this technology is the ability to "embed" compliance requirements into smart contract code. For example, a company's founders could directly write their commitment to "not resell their securities for a certain period" into the smart contract managing tokenized securities. Similarly, we can leverage blockchain to reimagine how issuers and holders communicate. Furthermore, privacy-preserving technologies such as zero-knowledge proofs could fundamentally change how we achieve the regulatory goals of the Bank Secrecy Act. In this model, Americans wouldn't have to surrender their privacy entirely to financial institutions, and these intermediaries would face lower compliance costs. Peirce: That sounds very promising. I've always been very concerned about how deeply embedded financial surveillance is in our financial system. Americans now have the opportunity to use new technologies to protect themselves from criminals while also protecting our nation from adversaries. We should seize this moment to re-evaluate the importance of financial privacy to the security of the American people. Now let's talk about the "elephant in the room": What are your thoughts on the recent decline in crypto asset prices? Should regulatory attention now be focused on this issue? Should regulators panic, or even care about the price drop? Atkins: Regulators' responsibility is not to worry about daily market fluctuations; our responsibility is to ensure market participants have the disclosure information they need to make informed investment decisions. Whether buying stocks, precious metals, or crypto assets, if someone's only focus is "the numbers always go up," they are likely to be disappointed. Markets rise and fall due to a variety of factors. As regulators, the most important thing we can do is ensure that the rules governing the asset classes we regulate provide market participants with the necessary information to express their judgment and sentiment about the market through decisions such as whether to buy related assets. Peirce: I agree. "Number go down" is a popular slogan these days, with some crypto critics even "taking to the streets to celebrate." In German, this reaction could be called "Schadenfreude," roughly translated as "schadenfreude"—taking pleasure in the loss or destruction of others. Here, we might call their attitude "Ethbelowthreeglee" (the jubilation of Ethereum falling below 3,000) or "Bitcoinunderseventylevity" (the exhilaration of Bitcoin falling below 70,000). But the best response to these critics isn't frantically searching for some regulatory change to make "the numbers go back up." Of course, providing clearer rules through legislation and regulation can help create an environment conducive to building value. But regulation is not the "source" of value creation. You have to create what people truly want and truly need. Only then can you gain broader support across both parties in Washington—if people are actually using something, the government will be less willing to take it away. Mr. Chairman, could you share some lessons learned from your years of experience in the capital markets on how innovators can interact more effectively with the regulatory system and successfully advance compliance and innovation? Atkins: I agree with you. In Washington, building things that people truly want and need speaks volumes. If this technology is developed and applied carefully, it could have a transformative impact on the financial system as securities are gradually put on-chain. For example, asset tokenization could change the financial system we know by shortening settlement cycles, facilitating the flow of collateral and dividends, enabling proxy voting, or making it easier for people to build and manage “customized, decentralized” portfolios. We are ready to partner with entrepreneurs committed to building a better future. I'm reluctant to repeat the often-ridiculed slogan of the previous administration, but I'd still like to say: "Come in and talk to us." We won't "favor" any particular asset or technology, nor will we act as your advocate, but we want our markets to remain open to those offering new products and services. Our regulatory framework should not be an obstacle to innovation—especially when such innovation further achieves our regulatory goals of protecting investors, promoting capital formation, and maintaining fair, orderly, and efficient markets. Peirce: You've struck a good balance. We're not cheerleaders for any new asset or technology, but we want markets to welcome those with ideas who are trying to improve how markets function. The SEC hasn't always been friendly enough. Improper regulation could deprive the American public of benefits they could otherwise enjoy. For example, our past reluctance to engage in constructive communication with token issuers has led to an anomaly: tokens that don't grant holders any substantial rights are less likely to attract negative regulatory attention than those that do. The consequence is that we now live in a world where most tokens don't grant their holders any rights. I hope we can reach a point where project developers are no longer afraid to design tokens that offer a claim on revenue streams and are therefore considered securities. Paul, what conditions and changes are needed to achieve a state where "people can confidently issue tokens that rightfully fall into the category of securities"? Atkins: We need to continue what we're doing—providing clearer rules and pathways on how tokenized securities fit into the existing regulatory framework and how intermediaries should operate compliantly when trading and custodian tokenized securities on behalf of clients. This work can only be done collaboratively; we welcome input from all sides, including those crypto opponents indulging in "Schadenfreude." I encourage everyone here to consider what attributes a token should possess to be truly useful to people; and then work with us to develop a regulatory framework that accommodates and supports these attributes without undermining our key regulatory objectives. Of course, this process takes time. Innovators don't have to wait for these changes to be fully implemented before they start building. While we engage in broader discussions and assess whether fundamental adjustments to the regulatory framework are necessary, communicating with us to see if there are viable compliance paths for existing rules within your specific circumstances and business structure may be a necessary transitional step. Peirce: Paul, you're known for your optimism even in challenging environments. Do you have any advice for listeners experiencing a difficult crypto market cycle on how to end this? Atkins: Keep a low profile and build what truly matters. That's how you transform "Schadenfreude" into "Freudenfreude"—the genuine joy we feel when others succeed. A little dark chocolate and Diet Coke might help, but things like Celsius and Zyn should be consumed in moderation.

data.title: Escaping the Gravity of Fiat Currency: I Don't Save Money, I Only Store "Purchasing Power"
data.description:
Author: Shirley; Source: X, @shirleyusy

An 18-Year Promise to Our Children: On Confidence, Cycles, and Choices. Also dedicated to every new parent.

After having children, money is no longer just numbers flashing on a screen, but a heavy responsibility.

To fulfill this responsibility, many friends have asked me, "Have you bought your child an education savings insurance policy? Or do you deposit a fixed amount into their account every month?" My answer might seem a bit harsh: "No. Because over an 18-year period, cash (fiat currency) may not be an asset, but rather a risk." This isn't about being unconventional; it's because the longer I trade in this market, the more "pessimistic" I become about wealth—I don't mean making money is difficult, but rather preserving purchasing power is extremely difficult. Today, I want to talk about the "cross-cycle growth fund" I've built for my child. This is not just an asset allocation list, but also the first financial literacy lesson I want to teach them.

I. The biggest risk is the illusion of "safety"

Our generation of parents is easily trapped in a kind of "nominal wealth" trap.

If you deposit 1 million yuan in the bank now, it seems very safe. But if we broaden our perspective to the macro-historical level, we will find a cruel truth: the purchasing power of fiat currency is destined to shrink.

The target inflation rate of major central banks around the world is usually 2%, which means that your money is "melting" every year. This is only the official data; the actual purchasing power loss in real life (medical care, education, core asset prices) is far greater than this figure.

Therefore, my first principle in establishing a fund for my children is not "preserving the principal," but "preserving the value."

What I want to fight is that invisible hand of inflation.

What I want to leave them is not a string of numbers destined to depreciate, but rather **real purchasing power** in this world. II. A Gift for the Future: Confidence (Fuck-you Money) Why go to such lengths? The goal of this fund is not to enable them to drive a luxury car when they turn 18, nor is it to allow them to live off their parents. I hope this asset will become their "confidence" as they grow up. This is perhaps something more important than money: the **right to refuse**. When they graduate from university and face a job they don't like but still need to pay rent, they can refuse and pursue their true dreams; when they encounter injustice and need to uphold their dignity, they don't have to bow down for a pittance. This freedom of "not being forced by life" is the best coming-of-age gift I can give them. Third, my core strategy: 50/30/20 allocation. To achieve this goal, I will not buy financial products that promise "stable returns." As a trader, in this turbulent AI era, I've built a more forward-thinking and resilient portfolio: 1. 50% Digital Gold (Bitcoin): An Anchor Across Cycles. This is the ballast of the entire portfolio. In a world of unlimited fiat currency issuance, what truly constitutes an anchor? The answer is: **digital assets with a fixed total supply and unalterable value.** I allocate 50% of my funds to Bitcoin. This is more than just an investment; it's an upgrade to "savings technology." It's insurance against the collapse of the fiat currency system, the gold standard of this digital age. 2. 20-30% Bet on the Dual Engines of Human Technology (QQQ + STAR Market 50) This is my core reason for being optimistic about the future: I believe technology is the only cure for cyclical upheavals. However, I won't put all my eggs in one basket. To cope with the complex geopolitical landscape of the future, I've constructed a "dual-engine" combination: Main Engine: Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) Its logic is "global vision." We must embrace the most advanced AI productivity of all humanity. Regardless of who leads the next industrial revolution, this index will automatically filter out the greatest companies of that era (such as Nvidia and Microsoft currently). Buying it is buying into the "dividends of global technological evolution." **Supporting Engine: STAR 50** Its logic is "independent and controllable." As Chinese families, we cannot ignore the resilience of our land. In the fields of semiconductors, high-end manufacturing, and biomedicine, China's "hard technologies" are undergoing a difficult breakthrough from 0 to 1. Allocating this portion reflects both optimism about the huge potential of domestic substitution and hedging against the potential risk of "technology decoupling" in the future. This offensive and defensive strategy forms a technological moat for our children's future. **3. 10-20% Last Resort (Gold): An Ark for Extreme Situations** While I am optimistic about digital assets, as a family fund, we must consider extreme risks (such as geopolitical conflicts, cyber black swan events, and other force majeure events). This 10-20% physical gold is the last line of defense, ensuring that in any extreme event, the child still has the resources to restart their life. Fourth, Silent Education: Be an Ally of Time. Many parents worry: "What if my child doesn't have financial literacy in the future?" Instead of lecturing them, let them see it for themselves. The operation of this fund is itself a "practical course" I designed for them spanning 18 years. I want to subtly teach them three things through this account: Regarding patience: I will show them that this account has experienced countless 30% and even 50% drawdowns over 18 years. But as long as the logic is correct, volatility is not a risk, but rather the "toll" we pay to time. Only by enduring loneliness can one enjoy prosperity. Regarding cognition: I will tell them why Mom dared to buy when "everyone was panicking," and why she remained calm when "everyone was celebrating." Investing is not about profiting from price differences, but about realizing the value of one's understanding. Regarding delayed gratification: In this era saturated with short videos and instant gratification, the ability to hold onto a stock for over 10 years is an extremely rare quality. In conclusion, the current "TA" (investor/investor) is just a little baby who can cuddle in my arms. It doesn't understand candlestick charts or Federal Reserve policies. But I know that every "buy" button I press today, every "sell" I refrain from doing today, is sending a signal to the future "TA". Investing is a journey of self-cultivation, and children are our best practice partners. The hope is that 18 years from now, when that person receives the private key to this account, they will inherit not only wealth, but also the wisdom to navigate life's cycles and the freedom to choose their own path.

data.title: Galaxy Digital: Developing the AI ​​Agent Capital Market
data.description:
Author: Lucas Tcheyan, Researcher at Galaxy Digital; Source: Galaxy Digital; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Introduction

The debate surrounding cryptocurrency and artificial intelligence (AI) is often framed as a relevance question: 'Does artificial intelligence need cryptocurrency?' This framework ignores what has already happened in practice.

A more important question is, 'What kind of economic infrastructure will AI systems need when they begin to operate autonomously, interact, transact, and create value without human intervention?'

Beyond experimentation with theoretical concepts like decentralized training, we've seen practical applications of cryptocurrencies and AI agents demonstrating their suitability as the economic foundation of an agent economy. In the crypto space, narrative-driven metasystems often spark centralized experimentation. On-chain AI agents have experienced two distinct waves of adoption. In late 2024, the first generation of agents rapidly gained popularity, with related tokens skyrocketing in value from zero to billions of dollars within months. Unsurprisingly, these projects were highly speculative. Even those aiming to provide genuine infrastructure or automation largely failed to consistently capture value or find genuine market demand. This January, the second wave of on-chain agent activity began to take shape. This wave reflects a maturation in token economic model design, participant characteristics, and technological goals. Agents have evolved from online virtual characters into real software systems such as tools, workflows, and services. The developers driving this shift are largely not native to the cryptocurrency space, but rather AI developers turning to on-chain fundraising. While token pricing still largely depends on market perception, cryptocurrency funding channels have once again demonstrated their effectiveness in capital formation, allocation, and monetization. These waves collectively demonstrate that the value proposition of cryptocurrencies for artificial intelligence is being tested in real time. This is crucial because autonomous agents are not merely applications, but economic agents. Systems built to support them must inherently possess the ability to coordinate, finance, and exchange value. Although much of the activity is currently speculative, this does not negate the signals it sends. Even under the most volatile conditions, the agent wave demonstrates that cryptocurrencies are the most viable economic foundation for autonomous systems. "Truth Terminal" and the AI ​​Agent Bubble of 2024 In mid-2024, researcher Andy Ayrey unveiled "Truth Terminal." He constructed this AI agent by allowing two Claude Opus agents to communicate with each other in an unsupervised manner. Venture capitalist Marc Andreessen donated $50,000 worth of Bitcoin to the project to observe its performance. Ayrey's work used indecent content from the internet as material, resulting in a model obsessed with a vulgar meme. The project began promoting a token called Goatseus Maximus (GOAT) on the X platform. This token had no practical use, no development roadmap, and no traditional team. It was launched by an anonymous developer on the Pump.fun platform. Even so, GOAT reached a market capitalization of $700 million in just two weeks. Within a month, its market capitalization surpassed $1 billion, becoming the first token on the Pump.fun platform to reach this milestone. Truth Terminal's success as a promoter is instructive. A semi-autonomous agent successfully attracted a large amount of speculative capital simply by grabbing attention. Ultimately, because it had no connection to the meme's intrinsic value (even if negligible), GOAT failed to achieve much success. This is not uncommon for Memecoin. However, it sparked an explosive growth in on-chain proxies. In the first wave, these proxies could be broadly categorized into three overlapping archetypes: Social platforms like GOAT, Luna, and Zerebro, driven by personality, leveraged virality to create demand. While they effectively attracted attention and funding, they didn't generate sustainable economic output. Analytics tools like AIXBT and Brain monitored cryptocurrency-native data streams and provided traders with insightful analytics. AIXBT amassed over 450,000 followers on the X platform, and its token market capitalization once approached $700 million. Early infrastructure and orchestration layers, such as Griffain, ARC, and Swarms, proposed frameworks for agent coordination, but these frameworks largely remained theoretical and failed to attract sustained use. Meanwhile, early launch platforms found a product-market fit by simplifying token issuance and liquidity. Platforms like Virtuals and ai16z enabled rapid market deployment, solidifying cryptocurrencies' advantage in guiding the development of autonomous agents. Capital formation mechanisms worked, but value accumulation failed. Tokens were narrative-driven, providing exposure to ideas or figures rather than actionable claims to underlying value. Few truly durable products emerged. Viral spreads entertained the masses but did not create economic value, and infrastructure projects lacked demand. The bubble truly validated the ability of cryptocurrencies to rapidly financialize software, one of the many components of emerging internet capital market theory. As we discussed earlier, this capability is the core value of general-purpose blockchains that host crypto assets. They create new assets, markets, and coordination mechanisms, providing an environment where anyone (or now, anything) can deploy code, issue assets, and iterate without permission. The bursting of the first agent bubble in early 2025 did not end the experiment. While speculative activity gradually subsided, long-term builders recognized not only that AI agents would exist for a long time, but also that blockchain was a crucial foundation for their applications. During 2025, existing solutions continued to evolve, while new on-chain agent primitives began to emerge: Launchpad's capital formation mechanism matured. Specifically, Launchpad realized that selling tokens to raise development funds was not a good strategy, especially when the tokens themselves did not provide any actual ownership. Virtuals replaced its one-size-fits-all Genesis model with three distinct launch categories, each designed for participants at different stages, from early experimentation to structured institutional access. A broader trend has shifted across all Launchpad platforms, with teams like Believe, Clanker, and Bags.FM introducing creator fee models. These Launchpads are beginning to allocate a portion of transaction fees directly to accounts associated with token issuance, rather than requiring them to sell tokens to raise development funds. Stablecoins have become a viable settlement layer for agents. The passage of the US GENIUS Act in July 2025 established a regulatory framework for stablecoins. AI agents require a natively digital medium of exchange that can operate continuously, globally, and without human intervention. Stablecoins provide agents with a programmable, USD-denominated medium of exchange that requires no bank account, Know Your Customer (KYC) verification, or credit card. Moreover, unlike Memecoins, stablecoins typically guarantee their value.

  • On-chain proxy payment standards converge with off-chain tools. In 2024 and 2025, major AI companies released significant autonomous proxy coordination layers, most notably Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) for tool access and Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol for cross-platform communication. But when it came to adding payment functionality, they didn't shy away from cryptocurrency integration. In September, Google launched the Proxy Payments Protocol (AP2), offering stablecoins and cryptocurrencies as native payment options alongside credit cards and bank transfers, and integrating Coinbase's x402 protocol as a stablecoin settlement layer usable in production environments.

    • These developments have laid the foundation for a new wave of on-chain proxy applications. All that's needed is a catalyst.

    These developments have laid the foundation for a new wave of on-chain proxy applications. All that's needed is a catalyst.

    Ralph Wiggum and Gas Town Spark the Second Agent Bubble In January 2026, AI agent technology reached its second turning point, this time not from crypto-native experiments, but from the widely used open-source tools built around Anthropic's AI coding assistant, Claude Code. Ralph Wiggum (developed by Geoffrey Huntley) and Gas Town (developed by Steve Yegge) became two of the most widely used open-source agent workflows. Ralph Wiggum (named after a character from the animated television series *The Simpsons*) introduced a simple way to enable AI coding agents to run continuously, handling complex tasks for extended periods without human supervision. Gas Town (named after the movie *Mad Max*) extended this, enabling the simultaneous coordination of multiple AI agents to collaborate on larger, more complex software projects. Crucially, both projects were developed as open-source software, and neither Huntley nor Yegge initially intended to commercialize their work through cryptocurrency. They were both initially skeptical of cryptocurrency as a funding mechanism. However, they ultimately benefited. A group of cryptocurrency enthusiasts quickly issued tokens associated with these two projects through Bags.fm, leveraging its creator fee model to directly allocate a portion of the token transactions to the two developers. On the surface, this resembles the previous Meme coin model: narrative-driven token issuance, speculative trading, and rapid price discovery. Critics were quick to point out that this model was predatory or exploitative, arguing that it was speculative under the guise of creator monetization. This criticism was not unfounded. In both cases, the developers abandoned the tokens, causing price crashes, highlighting a long-standing flaw in token-based funding models: the lack of clear definition regarding ownership, governance, and the alignment of long-term interests.

    Published by the creator of Ralph. Source: https://x.com/GeoffreyHuntley/status/2011737319402078383

    Excerpted from an article by the founder of Gas Town.

    Excerpted from an article by the founder of Gas Town.

    But as excerpts from the posts of the two founders above show, they recognized that cryptocurrency is not only a viable financing mechanism, but a superior one. I encourage readers to read both articles, as they are the best examples of changing the minds of those who know little about or are hostile to cryptocurrency. Of course, economic factors cannot be ignored. Both had the opportunity to profit from it, but this was not the only reason that prompted them to embrace cryptocurrency.

    Both articles clearly illustrate the specific advantages of the crypto capital market:

    • Permissionless, passive access to funds. Funding is available without a business plan, grant application, or deliberate commercialization. Funds flow automatically based on market activity and perception. **Programmable, low-friction settlement:** Creator royalties are automatically executed and settled instantly, a stark contrast to the manual, delayed, and opaque processes of traditional banking systems. **Aligned with open-source values:** Cryptocurrency funding enables developers to continue open-source development without equity dilution, licensing restrictions, or platform monopolies. **Market-based signaling:** Trading activity acts as a decentralized signal, indicating interest and relevance, similar to its role in the public stock market, helping to identify and fund projects amidst the explosive growth of AI-generated products. Scalability Beyond Traditional Funding Models. The cryptocurrency market provides funding for individual creators in ways that venture capital, grants, and sponsorships cannot match, especially against the backdrop of the increasing number of independent developers driven by artificial intelligence. Global Reach. Funding operates across borders, without relying on local banking infrastructure, enabling creators to access funding wherever they are. While tokens associated with Ralph Wiggum and Gas Town ultimately went to zero, this event is still a significant improvement over the proxy bubble of 2024. The crypto capital market provides funding for truly widely adopted open-source tools built by non-crypto native developers. Meanwhile, these cases reflect the role of cryptocurrencies in funding agency frameworks, rather than empowering agents to operate as autonomous economic agents. Bridging this gap—from funding agent development to empowering agents with the ability to autonomously trade, profit, and coordinate economically—will define the next phase of on-chain agent economies. Realizing Agent Capital Markets These two agent bubbles (the second may still be underway) demonstrate that blockchain infrastructure and crypto capital formation are well-suited for AI-powered agent systems, but also show that current implementations are far from perfect. Humans remain intermediaries. Agents still rely on humans to create and manage wallets, deploy tokens, approve transactions, and handle special cases. True economic autonomy has not yet been achieved. **Token Design Lacks Ownership Attributes:** Most proxy-related tokens do not offer any enforceable claims to cash flow, governance, or underlying output, resulting in a significant disconnect between their value and the proxy's output. **Product-Market Fit and Actual Demand Remain Weak:** Most economic activity still stems from trading and speculation rather than ongoing payments for proxy services, making profitability difficult even for businesses not focused on cryptocurrencies. **Interaction Between Proxy Agents is Still in its Early Stages:** While coordination frameworks are emerging, secure identities, permissions, reputation, and autonomous settlement between agents are still in their early stages and have not yet been widely validated. In conclusion, these shortcomings should be viewed as developmental issues, not directional deviations, and these issues are being actively addressed. It would be a grave mistake to ignore these innovations simply because they are closely related to the less serious meme market. On-chain services such as Bankr are developing the infrastructure that allows agents to issue and manage their own tokens, transfer revenue directly to their own wallets, and automatically fund inference and operations. Meanwhile, ownership tokens are emerging as a more reliable alternative to governance tokens, introducing explicit economic claims that better link token value to the performance of agents or projects. Agent payment standards such as x402, combined with identity and reputation frameworks such as ERC-8004, further enable agents to programmatically transact, coordinate, and hire each other, opening doors to applications beyond purely crypto-native use cases, even amidst fierce competition from fintech giants. Beyond these bottom-up on-chain developments, top-down developments have also prompted core developers, thought leaders, and foundations to focus more urgently on cryptocurrency as the default layer for proxy interactions. Just last week, Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin outlined his latest views on "Ethereum as the economic layer for AI-related interactions." The most profound lesson learned over the past 18 months is that proxy economies, especially those driven by open-source developers, require an economic foundation with the same characteristics as the open-source software itself: permissionless participation, composability, and trusted neutrality. Agents must be able to hold accounts, conduct global transactions, and coordinate economically without the need for platform operator approval. Traditional financial systems can support proxy payments in controlled environments, but they still require permission, are subject to jurisdictional restrictions, and require intermediaries. Cryptocurrency is the only widely deployed system that combines programmable settlement, global interoperability, and an open execution environment, allowing any agent or service to participate by default.

data.title: OpenAI releases EVMbench: AI steals money on DeFi, hackers will be out of a job.
data.description:
It's often said that Crypto's attempt to piggyback on AI's success is like offering a warm face to a cold backside, but recently, with the crypto market cooling down, OpenAI launched EVMbench, exciting many in the crypto community!

What is EVMbench?

First of all, you read that right. EVM here does indeed refer to the Ethereum Virtual Machine, which is the underlying environment for Ethereum and most compatible chains to run smart contracts.

However, this time OpenAI's EVMbench is not a blockchain, nor is it an Ethereum upgrade; it's an AI testing tool. This EVMbench also has a deep connection with the crypto community, because in addition to OpenAI, the developers of this tool include the well-known crypto foundation Paradigm and the Web3 security auditing company OtterSec. Currently, EVMbench is published as a paper, but today I'll give you a more in-depth look and explain it to you. [Image of EVMbench file: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7434620_image3.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/https://img.jinse.com.cn/7434620_image3.png) [Image of EVMbench file: hsXqbsVE3ILDKfR4xf4TdvTbXE9cM6KPNcndFAFB.jpeg]( ... However, OpenAI believes that on-chain processes are particularly suitable as an AI evaluation environment because all data and information are verifiable on-chain. Then, they found hundreds of real-world projects in a real DeFi environment. These projects averaged one or two thousand lines of code and contained multiple contracts. So this EVM Bench (Bench implies a judge's bench) is where mainstream AI models (GPT/Gemini/Claude) do these three things on these projects: Discover vulnerabilities (Detect). leaf="">Patch the vulnerability

  • Exploit the contract to steal money

Then we'll score who did the best~

Auditing is bad, but stealing money is good!

Finally, the development team found that the AI ​​performed only moderately well in finding or patching vulnerabilities, but was very strong in its ability to exploit vulnerabilities to attack contracts!

In the end, the development team found that the AI ​​performed only moderately well in finding or patching vulnerabilities, but was very strong in its ability to exploit vulnerabilities to attack contracts!

Given known issues with contracts, less than half of the vulnerabilities can be discovered or fixed, which only qualifies as a "code auditor" of average skill.

However, as a hacker, the success rate of stealing money is over 70%!

Moreover, whether AI acts as a "chain hacker" or an "auditor," as long as sufficient computing power (the number of tokens output by AI) is provided, its performance can be significantly improved.

Results of Each AI Model

At this point, everyone is probably very curious about which model is the most powerful~
