data.title: 2026: The UK's Next Generation of Payments Infrastructure Sets Sail
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Why is the UK building a new generation of payment infrastructure?

Driven by the global digital economy wave, the modernization of the payment system, as the lifeblood of the financial system, has become a core agenda for central banks around the world.In her speech at the 2026 Cities and Financial Payments Regulation and Innovation Summit, Sarah Breeden, Deputy Governor of the Bank of England, systematically elaborated on the UK's profound thinking and decisive actions in this area.The UK's determination to build the next generation of retail payment infrastructure is based on a comprehensive assessment of international trends, technological evolution, the limitations of existing systems, and future economic needs. First, international competitive pressure is the direct driving force. The UK was once a pioneer in global payment innovation, launching Faster Payments (FPS) in 2008, enabling 24/7 instant interbank retail payments. However, in the past decade, the pace of innovation in other economies has accelerated significantly. Systems like India's UPI, Brazil's Pix, and Sweden's Swish not only enable instant transfers but also deeply integrate into daily consumption scenarios, providing account-to-account (A2A) payment options based on mobile numbers and QR codes. This effectively reduces merchant costs and promotes financial inclusion. If the UK stagnates, its leading position as an international financial center will be challenged. Secondly, technological evolution has made upgrading payment functions possible. Emerging technologies, such as distributed ledger technology (DLT) and tokenization, are reshaping the way value is transferred. They enable a higher degree of customization, conditionality, and automation in payments than traditional direct debit and standing orders. For example, smart contracts can support automatic payment triggering after goods are signed for; this "programmability" is crucial for supporting an increasingly digital real economy (such as the Internet of Things and AI-powered agent payments). The UK must embrace these technologies to maintain the sophistication and competitiveness of its payment system. Secondly, the existing system has functional shortcomings and room for efficiency improvement. Although the UK's Faster Payments system is efficient, consumers and merchants still have limited choices in point-of-sale (POS) and online payment scenarios, heavily relying on international card network organizations. This results in merchants, especially small merchants, bearing high payment acceptance costs (averaging about 0.6%, with small merchants incurring costs four times that of large merchants). These costs are ultimately passed on to consumers. Introducing new A2A payment options can disrupt the existing pattern, reduce total social costs through competition, and enhance the overall resilience of the system, providing a backup channel for dealing with operational disruption risks. Finally, the future trend of diversified monetary systems requires infrastructure with stronger interoperability and inclusivity. The future of payments will no longer be a single form of commercial bank deposit currency, but rather a "multi-currency ecosystem" that may include tokenized deposits, regulated systemic stablecoins, and even retail central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). The next-generation infrastructure must be able to seamlessly and securely connect these different forms of currency, ensuring that 1 pound is always equivalent to 1 pound, maintaining public trust in the currency—a core responsibility of the Bank of England in maintaining monetary and financial stability. II. What is the Next-Generation Payment Infrastructure, Its Characteristics, and Application Scenarios? The next-generation retail payment infrastructure envisioned by the UK is not a simple patch to the existing system, but a strategic platform following a completely new design philosophy, aimed at achieving a range of public policy objectives. Its core definition is: a resilient, fair, trustworthy, and competitive payment system that supports a multi-currency ecosystem and serves the real economy. This infrastructure has several key characteristics: Scalability: The system design must be able to adapt to future unpredictable growth in transaction volume and user scale, avoiding bottlenecks to innovation due to limitations in the technical architecture. Modular: The infrastructure itself should act like a technological "building block," flexibly integrating and combining various new functions and services without requiring frequent disruptive modifications to the core system. This provides a stable underlying platform for innovation in the private sector. Flexible: It should be able to adapt to evolving user needs, business models, and regulatory requirements, supporting digital payments in low-connectivity or connectionless environments, as well as future scenarios such as payments initiated by AI agents. Based on these characteristics, the new generation of infrastructure will give rise to a series of key application scenarios, significantly improving the user experience: Scenario 1: Account-to-Account (A2A) Payments Between Stores and Online This is the most intuitive change. In the future, UK consumers will be able to choose to pay retailers directly from their bank accounts when shopping in physical stores or online, as a supplement to credit and debit cards. This not only provides new payment options, but more importantly, it is expected to reduce transaction costs for merchants by introducing competition. When payment instructions are transmitted quickly and securely through the new infrastructure, combined with appropriate planning rules, business models, and consumer protection standards, a more efficient and lower-cost retail payment landscape will emerge.

Scenario 2: Seamless Exchange of Traditional Currencies and Tokenized Currencies

The core capability of the new infrastructure is to achieve "interoperability." It is designed as a neutral exchange platform capable of seamlessly connecting different types of currencies. For example, when a consumer uses a regulated systemic stablecoin for payment, the new infrastructure needs to be able to process the transaction, relaying the payment instruction from the stablecoin issuer to the retailer's bank, and ultimately completing the final settlement of central bank currency in the Bank of England's Real-Time Gross Settlement System (RTGS). This means that regardless of whether the funds are in the form of traditional deposits or tokenized assets, users can easily and securely complete cross-institutional payments as if transferring money within the same bank. This breaks down potential "walled gardens," ensuring competition and innovation in the digital currency world.

Scenario 3: Improving Cross-Border Retail Payments

By implementing the internationally recognized ISO 20022 messaging standard, the new infrastructure can achieve greater automation in payment information processing, reducing costly and time-consuming manual intervention, thereby lowering the cost and speed of cross-border payments. In the long term, it also enables the UK to explore interconnecting its retail payment system with systems in other countries (such as the EU, the US, or emerging markets), further reducing transaction costs by reducing intermediaries and optimizing currency conversion, responding to the G20's global initiative on improving cross-border payments.

III. How will the Bank of England build the next-generation payment infrastructure?

The UK's process of building a new generation of payment infrastructure is itself a pioneering case of institutional innovation. It abandoned the traditional model unilaterally led by the government or the market, and instead constructed a new governance model oriented towards public policy objectives and centered on public-private partnerships. The first step: Establishing a top-level strategy and a new institutional model. The UK government, through the "Payments Vision Executive Committee," formulated a national-level retail payments strategy, clarifying public policy objectives. The Bank of England, the Treasury, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), and the Payment Systems Regulation Authority (PSR) jointly participated, ensuring consistency and authority in the strategic direction. In this new model, the Bank of England was given a new key role – responsible for translating macro strategy into concrete new infrastructure. To this end, the Bank of England spearheaded the establishment of the Retail Payments Infrastructure Committee (RPIB), whose members include banks, building associations, merchants, fintech companies, and existing retail payment system operator Pay.UK and regulators. This committee serves as a core platform for pooling industry wisdom, coordinating the interests of all parties, and promoting project implementation. Step Two: Defining Objectives, Outcomes, and Design Principles Under strategic guidance, in November 2025, the UK authorities clarified the specific outcomes the new infrastructure aims to achieve: creating a resilient, fair, credible, and competitive payment system that serves the real economy. Based on this, three core design principles were established: scalability, modularity, and flexibility. The RPIB will use these principles to accelerate the design of specific infrastructure solutions and plans to release a consultation document in the spring of 2026 to solicit public opinion. The third step: Encouraging parallel innovation during construction. Given that the construction of next-generation infrastructure takes time, the Bank of England has not adopted a "pause and wait" strategy, but has actively taken measures to encourage responsible innovation in the private sector in the short term. Technology Experimentation Platforms: The Bank of England has established the "Digital Pound Lab" and the "Synchronization Lab." The former allows businesses to test new retail payment use cases, such as payments using phone numbers or QR codes; the latter allows businesses to test how to integrate fund or asset flows on external ledgers with fund transfers between central bank accounts. These experiments provide a valuable practical environment for the industry to refine concepts and test systems, and also provide input for the final infrastructure design. Adaptive Regulation: The Bank of England has clarified its regulatory approach for new financial market infrastructures (FMIs). Once approved, a new payment system can enter the "mobilization" phase, launching under low-activity restrictions. As it scales up and risk characteristics become clearer, it gradually transitions to the "scale-up" phase and meets mature regulatory expectations. This phased, prudent regulation provides a safe space for innovation. The fourth step: Simultaneously advancing the modernization of the wholesale sector. The transformation of retail payments is not an isolated process. The Bank of England is also deeply innovating in the wholesale payments sector. A modernized RTGS system provides a platform for central bank monetary settlement of tokenized transactions. Through the Digital Securities Sandbox (DSS), it supports the private sector in establishing trading and settlement systems for tokenized securities. The Bank of England also actively participates in international collaborations such as the Bank for International Settlements' "Agora Project," exploring the feasibility of multiple central banks and private financial institutions jointly building a tokenized cross-border payment system, and is committed to promoting a globally universal standard for distributed ledger technology platforms to avoid market fragmentation. IV. Lessons from China's Development of Next-Generation Payment Infrastructure China boasts a globally leading retail payment system, with mobile payment penetration and user experience ranking among the world's best. However, the UK still offers valuable lessons in terms of strategic planning, governance models, and forward-looking technological deployment for future-oriented payment infrastructure. First, strategic foresight and systematic planning. The UK's reforms are not merely aimed at addressing current pain points, but are based on predictions of monetary forms, technological trends, and user needs 10-20 years from now. While China possesses powerful market forces like Alipay and WeChat Pay, it can learn from the UK's approach in the overall planning and top-level design of the national-level "multi-currency ecosystem" (coexistence of central bank digital currencies, tokenized deposits, and stablecoins). China should proactively consider the evolution path of its future fiat currency system and technological infrastructure to ensure that its national financial infrastructure can support the long-term development of the digital economy. Secondly, a public-private partnership-based institutionalized governance model. The UK, through legislative authorization and the establishment of the multi-party Participation Reserve Bank (RPIB), organically combines public policy objectives with industry innovation capabilities. China's payment infrastructure (such as NetsUnion and UnionPay) also embodies public-private partnerships to some extent, but the UK's RPIB model focuses more on the future-oriented "design" and "build" phases. Its broad membership and transparent decision-making process help build consensus early on and reduce resistance to future implementation. China can explore establishing a more regular consultation and decision-making mechanism in major financial infrastructure upgrade projects, involving regulators, existing market giants, fintech startups, and end-users. Third, technology neutrality and scalability design. The UK emphasizes modularity and flexibility in its infrastructure, aiming to leave interfaces for unpredictable future technologies and applications. While pursuing efficiency and stability, China's financial infrastructure also needs to consider how to avoid technology lock-in and enhance its compatibility with new technologies such as distributed ledger technology and artificial intelligence. In particular, the UK's "synchronization" approach and pursuit of interoperability are worth learning from in terms of how to achieve interconnectivity between existing centralized and efficient systems and the emerging distributed ledger applications. Fourth, the art of balancing innovation and risk prevention. The Bank of England, while building next-generation infrastructure, provides space for short-term innovation through "laboratories" and "phased regulation," demonstrating its superb policy balancing ability. While strengthening its regulation of fintech, China can also learn from its refined operation of "regulatory sandboxes" and technology experimentation platforms to guide innovation in a direction that aligns with national strategies, making regulation a "booster" rather than a "brake" for the orderly development of innovation. Fifth, an international perspective and standards leadership. The UK actively participates in promoting the global cross-border payment standard (ISO 20022) and exploring multilateral central bank digital currency bridges, committed to maintaining its voice in global financial rule-making. Similarly, in promoting the internationalization of the RMB and the cross-border pilot program of the digital RMB, China also needs to deeply participate in the formulation and coordination of international payment standards, strengthen technical dialogue and cooperation with central banks and international organizations, and ensure that China's payment infrastructure can efficiently connect with the global system, contributing to the construction of an open world economy. In conclusion, the UK's blueprint for building a new generation of payment infrastructure is a profound and systematic transformation of financial infrastructure. It is not only about technological upgrades, but also about innovation in governance models, reshaping public policies, and a far-reaching layout for the future form of currency. For China, which is moving towards a stage of high-quality development, in-depth research and learning from its experience will help us, on the basis of consolidating our existing advantages, to plan and build a truly future-oriented, secure, efficient, inclusive, and competitive national payment infrastructure with a more forward-looking vision, more scientific governance, and a more open mindset.

data.title: Analysis of Financial Market Volatility in 2026: Hidden Concerns and Historical Echoes of the AI ​​Boom
data.description:
At the beginning of 2026, global financial markets exhibited significant volatility, bearing many similarities to the dot-com bubble of 2000. At that time, the tech-driven market boom ultimately ended in a dramatic crash, causing the Nasdaq to plummet by 78%. Today, the growth model driven by AI (artificial intelligence) technology is facing a similar test. Market data shows that the S&P 500 rose 1.5% in January, but its internal structure was clearly divergent, with large-cap tech stocks performing poorly, while defensive sectors such as utilities and consumer staples were relatively resilient. This rotation reflects investors' concerns about the economic growth outlook, accompanied by warning signs such as the Hindenburg Omen, suggesting potential downside risks. This article analyzes the causes of the current volatility based on the latest market data and provides relevant commentary and outlook.

Market Overall Performance and Historical Comparison

At the start of 2026, the S&P 500 index continued its strong momentum from the previous three years, with a 1.5% return in January, pushing the index close to the 7,000-point mark. According to Goldman Sachs' forecast, the index's total return for the year is expected to reach 12%, a slowdown from 18% in 2025, but still above the historical average. This is thanks to strong corporate earnings growth expectations, with analysts predicting that earnings per share growth for non-“Magnificent 7” stocks will double to 14%-16%. However, this optimism is not without its challenges.

Market Overall Performance and Historical Comparison

Initially, the S&P 500 index continued its strong momentum from the previous three years, with a 1.5% return in January, pushing the index close to the 7,000-point mark. According to Goldman Sachs' forecast, the index's total return for the year is expected to reach 12%, a slowdown from 18% in 2025, but still above the historical average. This is thanks to strong expectations for corporate earnings growth, with analysts predicting that earnings per share growth for non-“Magnificent 7” stocks will double to 14%-16%.

Flow data shows that short interest in tech stocks has surged and is expected to rise throughout 2026. Accelerated IPOs (such as SpaceX and OpenAI) are similar to private equity cashing out during the dot-com era. Defensive stocks such as consumer staples are seeing increased long positions. Key levels: S&P 500 support at 6800, resistance at 7000; Nasdaq support at 600. A break below these levels could lead to a 10%-15% pullback.

Consumer and Economic Weakness

Slowing consumer spending is a potential risk. December retail sales were flat, below the expected 0.4% growth (Reuters). Core retail sales fell 0.1%, suggesting a slowdown in consumer spending from 3.5% in Q3. Raymond James noted that weak employment is driving sentiment: job growth in 2025 is projected to be the second worst since the financial crisis. Dallas Fed data shows that high-income groups account for 57% of consumption, while trade among low-income groups is downgrading. The labor market is cooling: January nonfarm payrolls exceeded expectations, but overall growth slowed. The unemployment rate remained stable at 4.4%, but youth unemployment rose (15.7% for 16-19 year olds). Forrester predicts that in 2026, technology investment will mask weakness in other capital expenditures, and consumer polarization will intensify. The expiration of ACA subsidies will increase the burden on low-income households, dragging down consumption (accounting for two-thirds of GDP).

Key Fed Policy: January rate maintained at 3.5%-3.75%, with rate cuts expected starting in June. J.P. Morgan expects no recession, but weak employment may force further easing. Inflation is stubborn (core above 2%), limiting the room for rate cuts.

Cryptocurrency and Commodity Dynamics

The crypto market fear index reached 5 (CoinMarketCap), Bitcoin fell to $60,000, and its market capitalization dropped by 22%. BeInCrypto points out that extreme panic is often a buy signal, with a rebound after historical lows. However, the cycle may be prolonged until 2026, and the sustainability of mining is declining.

Commodity volatility was high: gold fell to $4,761/oz (-2%), silver to $75 (-9.7%), and oil to $62 (-3%). Reuters reported that easing tensions with Iran triggered selling pressure, but supply risks remain. J.P. Morgan forecasts silver at an average of $81/oz, benefiting from industrial demand (solar energy). Amundi points out that copper faces structural shortages (electrification, AI), and oil prices are susceptible to geopolitical influences. Historical data shows that commodity bear markets are often accompanied by stock market corrections. Capital Economics predicts oil prices will reach $50 by the end of the year, while gold is supported by central bank buying. Analysis, Commentary, and Outlook The current volatility is not a doomsday sign, but rather a repricing event. Concerns about an AI bubble are reasonable, but excessive pessimism may lead to missed opportunities. History shows that corrections after periods of high valuations often lead to recovery: the S&P 500's four-day winning streak is rare, but it is projected to grow by 2.4% in 2026 (above trend). Defensive rotation suggests a soft landing, with Fed easing providing a buffer. Risks include: delayed AI returns, geopolitical escalation, and a rebound in inflation. Opportunities lie in: small-cap stock rallies and buying commodities at low points. Investors should diversify: 40% in stocks, 30% in bonds, 10% in commodities, and 20% in cash. Monitor the VIX and Hindenburg, and avoid leverage. Looking ahead to 2026, volatility may persist into Q1-Q2, recovering in Q3-Q4. S&P targets are 7500-8100, with Bitcoin potentially reaching $150,000 (if fears subside). However, a 34% pullback (similar to 2000) is not impossible if key levels are broken. Balancing risk and opportunity is crucial.

data.title: The White House held its third meeting on stablecoins; Nvidia is about to invest in OpenAI.
data.description:
Headline

▌White House Holds Third Closed-Door Meeting on Stablecoins, Crypto Executives Say "Further Progress Expected"

The White House held its third closed-door meeting on Thursday, inviting representatives from the crypto industry and banking groups to discuss stablecoin reward mechanisms. The meeting lasted several hours and was described by attendees as a "constructive dialogue," but no final compromise was reached. Ji Hun Kim, CEO of the Crypto Council for Innovation, stated that the meeting further advanced the framework based on previous discussions, aiming to enhance U.S. competitiveness while protecting consumers. Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paul Grewal also described the discussions as "cooperative and constructive," hinting that further negotiations would continue. Stablecoin holding rewards mechanisms are a key point of contention in current crypto market structure legislation. Sources familiar with the matter revealed that the White House proposed some principles allowing companies to offer rewards based on specific activities or account balances, but banks remain cautious. Whether a next round of meetings will be held is yet to be determined.

Participating organizations included Ripple, the Blockchain Association, the Crypto Council for Innovation, and several major banking associations.

▌Nvidia Closely Agreeing to $30 Billion Investment in OpenAI

According to the Financial Times: Nvidia is close to finalizing a $30 billion investment in OpenAI, replacing the $100 billion long-term commitment reached between the two companies last year.

▌Coinbase CLO: Constructive Dialogue with White House Crypto Advisor at the Third Stablecoin Yields Conference

Coinbase Chief Legal Officer Paulgrewal.eth posted on the X platform that he made further progress today in his communication with Patrick Witt, Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Digital Assets, at the White House. The dialogue was constructive, and the attitude was cooperative and positive. More actions are expected to follow.

▌Wu Jiezhuang Suggests Issuing Nighttime Electronic Consumption Vouchers to Promote Stablecoin Applications; Costs to be Beared by Stablecoin Issuers Licensed Next Month

Hong Kong Legislative Councilor Wu Jiezhuang proposed to take advantage of the opportunity of issuing the first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses in Hong Kong next month to launch "nighttime electronic consumption vouchers," distributed through e-wallets, to promote the nighttime economy, support businesses, and promote stablecoins.

Wu Jiezhuang emphasized that the plan is not an "airdrop" of money to the general public, nor is it a government-funded arrangement; the relevant administrative costs can be borne by the licensed stablecoin issuing institutions. The Ethereum Foundation released its 2026 protocol priority update, outlining three tracks: scaling (integrating L1 execution and Blob scaling), improving user experience (focusing on native account abstraction and cross-chain interoperability), and strengthening the L1 layer (enhancing security, censorship resistance, and network resilience). Furthermore, the Ethereum Foundation stated that it will continue to push for increasing the Gas Limit to 100M and above, advancing ePBS and further adjusting Blob parameters, advancing the zkEVM attester client, and working on censorship resistance and post-quantum security. The next major upgrade to Glamsterdam is targeted for the first half of 2026, with Hegotá planning to follow up later this year. The Ethereum Foundation announced the formation of a Platform Team, aiming to build a unified platform that leverages the advantages of the Ethereum L1+L2 combination. The goal is to build the most powerful Ethereum platform possible, enabling L1 and L2 layers to better support users, applications, and all organizations built on Ethereum. The aim is to improve the relationship between the L1 and L2 layers, creating a mutually reinforcing system across all layers. The Foundation stated that since first proposing the Rollup-centric roadmap five years ago, a chain ecosystem has developed around the Ethereum L1 layer. The early Rollup mindset has been replaced by differentiated L2 layer networks, each with its own unique and valuable economic system, extending Ethereum's core features to millions of users. As Ethereum matures, the Foundation believes further steps are needed to build a unified platform that fully leverages the unique strengths of the Ethereum system (L1 + L2). This includes enhancing the value proposition of L2, optimizing L2 adoption mechanisms to create value more broadly for Ethereum, guiding the ecosystem towards a more secure, permissionless architecture, and simplifying the adoption process for users and institutions. More importantly, building a robust Ethereum means driving technological improvements to strengthen and enhance Ethereum's core features and unique advantages. Kraken announced a new integration with ICE Chat, the instant messaging platform of the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), enabling institutional traders to connect directly with Kraken's over-the-counter (OTC) trading division through this widely used communication network. This move integrates Kraken's OTC execution capabilities into the institution's existing workflows, lowering the entry barrier and improving access to cryptocurrency liquidity in the spot and options markets. Three researchers from the Federal Reserve System published a paper on February 12th, arguing that data from the prediction market platform Kalshi can reflect macroeconomic expectations more in real time and should be included in the Fed's decision-making framework. The authors include Federal Reserve Chief Economist Anthony Diercks, Research Assistant Jared Dean Katz, and Johns Hopkins University researcher Jonathan Wright. The study compared Kalshi data with traditional surveys and market-implied expectations, concluding that Kalshi can provide high-frequency, continuously updated probability distributions to measure the risk-neutral probability of macroeconomic indicators such as interest rate decisions. The researchers noted that Kalshi reacts more quickly after major policy statements or economic data releases. However, this Fed research paper is for internal discussion and does not represent a formal policy stance.

▌Uniswap has launched a proposal to increase protocol fees

Uniswap announced on X that a proposal to increase protocol fees has been launched. This update proposes to enable fees for all remaining v3 liquidity pools on the mainnet and to enable protocol fees for v2 and v3 on the eight newly added chains.

Cryptocurrency

▌Bloomberg: Despite recent outflows, the US spot Bitcoin ETF still recorded a net inflow of $53 billion

While the US spot Bitcoin ETF has recently experienced large-scale redemptions, the overall data presents a different picture.

According to Eric Balchunas, cumulative net inflows into Bitcoin ETFs peaked at $63 billion last October and are currently around $53 billion, even after months of redemptions. Balchunas wrote on the X platform, "Two years later, net inflows are still +$53 billion." The data he shared was compiled by analyst James Seyffart. This figure far exceeds Bloomberg's earlier predictions, which projected net inflows of $5 billion to $15 billion over two years. In other words, the recent outflows haven't erased the overall success story. Despite Bitcoin's roughly 50% drop from its high, institutional funds haven't flowed out significantly in tandem, indicating that many investors are choosing to hold for the long term rather than panic selling.

▌Wall Street Executives and Government Officials Attend Conference Hosted by Trump Family Crypto Platform

Wall Street executives and government officials gathered at the opulent Mar-a-Lago ballroom for a conference hosted by the Trump family's cryptocurrency platform—highlighting how digital assets have become a dual focus of policy and personal profit during Donald Trump's second term. David Solomon, CEO of Goldman Sachs Group, attended the event throughout Wednesday, completing his transformation from a crypto skeptic to a Bitcoin holder. He was joined by pardoned felon, billionaire, and Binance co-founder CZ. Attendees also included heads of the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq, as well as current and former financial regulators.

... According to Arkham data, the UAE has accumulated $453.6 million in Bitcoin through mining with its partner Citadel. It is reported that they have retained most of their Bitcoin production, with the last recorded outflow of funds four months ago. After deducting energy costs, the UAE's current profit on its Bitcoin holdings is $344 million.

▌Michael Saylor: We hope Strategy can become a company that changes the world

In an interview, Strategy founder Michael Saylor stated, "We aspire to be a company that provides comfortable retirement for a billion people and changes the world, and we hope to change the monetary system."

▌Morgan Stanley and other major shareholders increased their Bitmine stock holdings during the sell-off

Despite the overall collapse of the cryptocurrency market and poor stock performance, Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR)'s largest shareholder increased its investment in the leading Ethereum fund management company in the fourth quarter of 2025. According to Morgan Stanley's 13F filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, as the largest shareholder, Morgan Stanley increased its stake by approximately 26%, holding more than 12.1 million shares at the end of the quarter, worth approximately $331 million.

▌Federal Reserve's Daly says labor market improved after 75 basis point rate cut

Federal Reserve's Daly stated that labor market conditions improved after the 75 basis point rate cut.

▌94.1% probability of the Fed keeping rates unchanged in March

According to CME's "FedWatch": The probability of the Fed cutting rates by 25 basis points by March is 5.9%, and the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 94.1%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting rates by 25 basis points by April is 20.5%, the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 78.5%, and the probability of cumulative rate cuts of 50 basis points is 1.0%. The probability of cumulative rate cuts by 25 basis points by June is 49.8%.

▌Federal Reserve's Daly says labor market improved after 75 basis point rate cut

Golden Encyclopedia

▌What are Long and Short Positions?

Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline. Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and to capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions.

In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (usually borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: What exactly are stablecoins? Their institutional positioning, regulatory framework, and tax implications.
data.description:
Introduction

As the cryptocurrency market continues to expand, its internal trading structure is gradually adjusting. Stablecoins, due to their relatively stable value performance, are increasingly taking on pricing and settlement functions in cryptocurrency trading, cross-border transfers, and on-chain financial activities. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) points out that stablecoins have evolved from early market auxiliary tools into financial arrangements that may have cross-market and cross-institutional influence.

From a market structure perspective, the current stablecoin system is dominated by stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies, with US dollar stablecoins holding a dominant position.

As of February 12, 2026, the top two stablecoins by market capitalization were USDT ($183.9 billion) and USDC ($73.3 billion), far exceeding the third-ranked USDe ($6.3 billion). Research from the FSB and the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) shows that the expansion of stablecoins is closely related to global payment efficiency, the use of dollar-denominated assets, and the development of digital financial infrastructure. Stablecoins are gradually entering the research scope of financial regulation and macroeconomic policies. Functionally, stablecoins combine blockchain technology attributes with characteristics of payment tools. On the one hand, they rely on distributed ledger technology to achieve peer-to-peer transfers and 24/7 settlement; on the other hand, their value anchoring mechanism gives them stability approaching that of traditional currencies in specific scenarios. This structural feature has led to the widespread application of stablecoins in cross-border payments, crypto asset trading, and decentralized finance. As usage increases, regulatory agencies are gradually focusing their attention on the potential systemic impact of stablecoins. The FSB, BIS, and central banks of many countries have consistently emphasized in their reports that stablecoins may have spillover effects on payment systems, capital flows, and financial stability, and that their issuance, reserve management, and operational arrangements are of institutional significance. In some jurisdictions, stablecoins have been explicitly incorporated into the financial regulatory framework and are aligned with the regulatory rules for payment instruments, electronic money, or crypto assets. Against this backdrop, this paper focuses on the institutional positioning and operational logic of stablecoins within the existing financial system, analyzing their conceptual types, functional scenarios, market structure, and regulatory framework, and will also examine the tax issues related to stablecoin-related activities. 1. Definition and Classification of Stablecoins 1.1 Basic Concepts of Stablecoins Stablecoins are generally understood as a type of crypto-asset that maintains relative value stability through a specific mechanism. The FSB describes stablecoins as a digital value carrier issued based on distributed ledger technology, pegged to one or more assets to reduce price volatility. Unlike early crypto-assets that primarily served investment or speculative functions, stablecoins are designed with greater emphasis on payment, settlement, and value storage functions. From an institutional perspective, stablecoins are not equivalent to fiat currency; their value stability stems from contractual arrangements, reserve assets, or algorithmic mechanisms. For this reason, stablecoins typically occupy an intersection between traditional currencies, electronic currencies, and crypto assets, and their legal nature and regulatory classification exhibit differentiated treatment paths in different jurisdictions. 1.2 Distinguishing Stablecoins from Other Digital Currencies Within the digital currency system, it is necessary to distinguish stablecoins from other major forms. First, compared to central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), stablecoins are issued by private institutions, and their credit basis stems from the issuer and its asset arrangements, rather than sovereign credit. The BIS emphasizes that CBDCs are a digital form of sovereign currency, while stablecoins remain a financial arrangement provided by the private sector. Secondly, compared to highly volatile cryptocurrencies, the core objective of stablecoins is to reduce price uncertainty, making them more suitable as a medium of exchange and unit of account. When assessing the risks of stablecoins, regulatory agencies often differentiate them from highly volatile crypto assets such as Bitcoin, discussing them separately within the framework of payment regulation and financial stability. 1.3 Main Types of Stablecoins Based on different value stabilization mechanisms, existing research typically classifies stablecoins into the following categories: The first category is fiat-backed stablecoins. Common examples include USDT and USDC. These stablecoins use fiat currency or highly liquid financial assets as reserves, supporting their issuance size on a one-to-one or near-proportional basis. This type dominates the current market, and its operational logic and transparency requirements have become a key focus in the design of regulatory systems in various countries. The second type is other asset-backed stablecoins. Common examples include DAI and LUSD. These stablecoins use crypto assets or physical assets as collateral, and their value stability depends on collateral ratio management and liquidation mechanisms. Due to the volatility of the underlying asset prices, these stablecoins typically require over-collateralization and automatic liquidation arrangements to maintain overall stability. The third type is algorithmic stablecoins. Common examples include AMPL. These stablecoins do not rely on sufficient asset reserves but maintain price anchoring through algorithmic supply and incentive mechanisms. International organizations generally agree in their assessments that this model is highly sensitive to market confidence and mechanism design, and its stability and risk structure exhibit significant uncertainty. 2. Main Functions and Application Scenarios of Stablecoins 2.1 Transaction Pricing and Settlement Functions Within the crypto market, stablecoins primarily function as transaction pricing and settlement tools. Compared to cryptocurrencies, which experience significant price volatility, stablecoins, by being pegged to fiat currencies or other assets, provide a relatively stable unit of value during transactions. This characteristic makes them widely used as the basic unit of account for trading pairs in centralized exchanges and decentralized trading platforms. The use of stablecoins in the crypto market helps reduce the impact of price volatility on trading decisions and, to some extent, improves market liquidity and trading efficiency. In practice, the pricing, clearing, and risk management arrangements for a large number of crypto assets revolve around mainstream stablecoins, and their functions have gone beyond the scope of simply being auxiliary assets. 2.2 Cross-border Payments and Fund Transfers Another important application scenario for stablecoins is cross-border payments and fund transfers. Relying on blockchain networks, stablecoins can achieve peer-to-peer transfers and 24/7 settlement, reducing reliance on intermediaries at the technical level. The World Bank has repeatedly pointed out in its assessment of cross-border payment systems that existing cross-border payment processes still have room for improvement in terms of cost, speed, and transparency, and payment arrangements based on distributed ledger technology provide new possible paths for this. Stablecoins are seen by some market participants as one of the tools to improve the efficiency of cross-border payments. Regulatory agencies believe that such applications need to be aligned with anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, and capital flow management requirements, and their potential benefits coexist with compliance risks. 2.3 The Fundamental Role of Stablecoins in Decentralized Finance (DeFi) In the decentralized finance (DeFi) system, stablecoins typically function as underlying assets. Due to their relatively stable value, stablecoins are frequently used as collateral or pricing benchmarks in lending, liquidity provision, and derivatives trading. The Financial Stability Board (FSB) has noted in its research that stablecoins have become one of the most important value anchors in the DeFi ecosystem, and their stability directly affects the operation of related financial activities. From an institutional perspective, the widespread use of stablecoins in DeFi extends their risk impact from the level of a single product to the entire on-chain financial ecosystem. This characteristic has also become an important basis for regulatory agencies to assess the systemic impact of stablecoins. 2.4 Pricing and Settlement Tools in Real-World Asset Tokenization Besides native crypto scenarios, stablecoins are also used in arrangements related to Real-World Asset Tokenization (RWA). In some practices, stablecoins are used as pricing and settlement tools for tokenized asset issuance, trading, and profit distribution. BIS research points out that the role of stablecoins in RWA scenarios is mainly reflected in providing a unified value standard and on-chain settlement methods. Currently, the application of stablecoins in this field is highly dependent on legal, regulatory, and asset ownership arrangements; their functionality is closely related to compliance boundaries, and related practices are currently concentrated in specific jurisdictions and pilot environments. 3. Regulatory Framework and Major Jurisdictional Practices for Stablecoins 3.1 Regulatory Objectives and Basic Principles From an international perspective, the core objectives of stablecoin regulation focus on three aspects: first, maintaining payment systems and financial stability; second, preventing money laundering, terrorist financing, and other financial crimes; and third, protecting users' rights and improving information transparency. The FSB explicitly states that stablecoin arrangements should meet regulatory requirements commensurate with their risks in terms of issuance, reserve management, redemption mechanisms, and governance structures. 3.2 The United States: A Regulatory Path Centered on Payment-Oriented Stablecoins In the United States, stablecoin regulation has gradually revolved around payment-oriented stablecoins. Federal requirements stipulate that stablecoins issued to the public and used for payment settlements must be backed by high-quality liquid assets and subject to ongoing regulation. A joint report by the U.S. Treasury Department and relevant financial regulatory agencies points out that the business activities of stablecoin issuers are highly similar to those of traditional payment institutions, and their regulatory arrangements should be guided by financial stability and consumer protection. In terms of institutional design, the United States tends to incorporate stablecoins into the existing financial regulatory system by clearly defining the qualifications of issuers, reserve asset requirements, and information disclosure obligations. This approach reflects a functional regulatory approach. 3.3 The European Union: A Unified Regulatory Model Under the MiCA Framework The European Union has systematically regulated stablecoins in its Crypto-Asset Market Regulation (MiCA), incorporating them into a unified regulatory framework for crypto-assets. MiCA differentiates between different types of stablecoins and sets requirements for issuers regarding capital, governance, reserve assets, and information disclosure. The European Commission emphasized in its legislative documents that this framework aims to achieve a balance between promoting innovation and preventing systemic risks. From an institutional perspective, MiCA reduces compliance costs caused by regulatory fragmentation by establishing unified standards among member states, while simultaneously improving regulatory predictability. This model is considered a representative path for regional coordinated regulation in international discussions. 3.4 Hong Kong: Licensing and Centralized Regulatory Model Hong Kong has gradually established a dedicated regulatory system for stablecoins in recent years. According to policy documents issued by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), stablecoin issuers are required to obtain licenses and are subject to ongoing supervision regarding reserve asset management, redemption arrangements, and risk control. The relevant system clearly positions stablecoins as digital assets with payment functions and incorporates them into the monetary and payment stability framework. A significant feature of this model is centralized regulatory power and clear entry standards. Through the licensing system, regulators can screen stablecoin arrangements at the market entry stage, thereby controlling potential risks at the institutional level. 3.5 Regulatory Comparison and Common Trends A comprehensive comparison of practices across different jurisdictions reveals several common trends. First, all jurisdictions generally emphasize the importance of reserve asset quality and redemption mechanisms. Second, stablecoin issuers and key service providers are increasingly being brought under ongoing supervision. Third, the linkage between stablecoin regulation and frameworks for cross-border payments, anti-money laundering, and tax information exchange is strengthening. This demonstrates that stablecoins inherently possess cross-border attributes, and regulatory arrangements in a single jurisdiction cannot fully cover their risk spillover effects; therefore, strengthening international coordination has become an important direction for stablecoin regulation. 4. Tax Issues and Compliance Challenges of Stablecoins 4.1 Tax Attributes and Qualitative Issues of Stablecoins From a tax law perspective, the primary issue concerning stablecoins lies in the qualitative definition of their legal and tax attributes. Most national tax systems do not treat stablecoins as legal tender, but rather categorize them as crypto assets or virtual assets. The OECD points out that while stablecoins possess the characteristic of relatively stable prices, their issuance and circulation mechanisms do not alter their fundamental attribute as non-sovereign digital assets. This positioning directly impacts their tax treatment. Under the current framework, stablecoins are generally not subject to foreign exchange or currency-related tax rules, but are treated more like property assets or financial assets. Their transfer, exchange, or use for settling obligations may all constitute taxable events. 4.2 Key Tax Payment Stages for Stablecoin-Related Transactions From a practical perspective, stablecoins may trigger tax obligations at multiple stages. First, when stablecoins are exchanged for fiat currency or other crypto assets, tax authorities often focus on whether asset transfer gains are generated. The OECD considers the exchange of virtual assets for other assets or fiat currency as a disposal, and taxable income should be determined according to the domestic laws of each country. Second, when using stablecoins for payment settlement, the tax treatment depends on the nature of the transaction itself. If stablecoins are used to purchase goods or services, tax concerns typically focus on whether the underlying transaction constitutes a subject of value-added tax or a similar turnover tax. Furthermore, stablecoins are widely used in investment and financial activities for lending, staking, and profit distribution arrangements. A joint study by the BIS and FSB points out that related income should be distinguished from gains on asset price changes and interest, fees, or other forms of compensation for tax purposes, and classified according to economic substance. 4.3 Tax Issues for Stablecoin Issuers and Service Providers Beyond the user level, stablecoin issuers and related service providers also face specific tax issues. Stablecoin issuers typically generate income through reserve asset management, service fees, or related financial arrangements, and their tax treatment involves issues such as corporate income tax, withholding tax, and the application of cross-border tax treaties. In its research on digital economy taxation, the OECD emphasizes that the issuance and operation of virtual assets should be incorporated into the existing corporate tax framework and regulated by clarifying the standards for determining the source of income and permanent establishment. Furthermore, custody, clearing, and payment service providers in the stablecoin ecosystem may be considered financial intermediaries or digital service providers in different jurisdictions, and their tax obligations depend on their specific business models and regulatory positioning. 4.4 Tax Administration and Information Visibility Issues The widespread use of stablecoins presents new challenges to tax administration. Traditional tax systems rely on financial institution declarations and third-party information reports, while stablecoin transactions, with their decentralized and cross-border characteristics, increase the difficulty for tax authorities to obtain information. To address this issue, the OECD proposed the Crypto Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) based on the existing CRS framework, aiming to achieve automatic exchange of transaction and holding information through crypto asset service providers. CARF explicitly includes stablecoins in the reporting scope and requires eligible service providers to identify users, record transaction information, and report to tax authorities. The OECD believes that this framework helps improve tax transparency in virtual asset transactions and reduces opportunities for cross-border tax evasion. 4.5 Real-world Challenges of Stablecoin Tax Compliance Despite ongoing international institutional development, stablecoin tax compliance still faces several challenges in practice. First, the tax classification of stablecoins and related income is not yet fully unified across countries, potentially leading to double taxation or tax vacuums. Second, the connection between tax rules and financial regulatory rules needs improvement, especially in cross-border scenarios where information sharing and enforcement coordination are costly. Third, tax authorities need to strike a balance between technological capabilities, compliance costs, and taxpayer rights protection when applying the new reporting framework. Conclusion Overall, stablecoins have established a relatively stable functional position in the crypto asset market. Their applications in scenarios such as transaction pricing, cross-border payments, decentralized finance, and the tokenization of real-world assets reflect the real needs of the evolution of digital financial infrastructure. At the same time, stablecoins have not deviated from the existing financial system; their market structure, risk spillovers, and institutional impacts largely perpetuate and amplify the existing pattern of the current monetary and payment system. At the regulatory level, major jurisdictions are gradually forming institutional frameworks centered on functional regulation and risk orientation around stablecoins, showing a trend of convergence in terms of reserve asset management, issuer access, and ongoing supervision. In the tax field, although stablecoins possess price stability, they are still considered virtual assets under current international consensus, and their related transactions and returns are gradually entering the purview of tax administration and information exchange systems. With the advancement of institutional tools such as CARF, the compliance boundaries and visibility of stablecoins are expected to become clearer. The development of stablecoins reflects both the impact of technological innovation on financial operations and the ongoing constraints imposed by institutional factors such as financial stability, regulatory coordination, and tax compliance. Given the continuous improvement of relevant systems, ongoing and prudent institutional research on stablecoins is of practical significance.

data.title: Arthur Hayes: AI Triggers US Financial Crisis
data.description:
Author: Arthur Hayes

Editor's Note: This article has been slightly abridged

Any views expressed here are the author's personal opinions and should not be used as investment advice or trading recommendations.

When a fire alarm sounds in an apartment or office building, you usually ignore it. But for those who ignore obvious warning signs, death can sometimes strike unexpectedly. In this daily life filled with all sorts of noise, knowing which alarms to listen to can not only save your life but also your wallet.

If consumers can't afford their credit card payments, the next to suffer will be stocks in the non-essential consumer goods sector.

Financial institutions that specialize in serving white-collar workers facing bankruptcy, such as American Express (AXP US, white), can barely hold on when borrowers' employers (IGV US, gold) are battered by the market. However, as the market fully prices the future fate of knowledge workers, this resilience of lending institutions will be unsustainable.

When unsure about the adequacy of dollar liquidity, look at Bitcoin.

Nasdaq (gold) is consolidating, while Bitcoin (white) has plummeted since hitting an all-time high in October 2025.

Gold (gold) is surging while Bitcoin (white) is plunging. This divergence clearly tells us: A deflationary credit crisis is brewing within the United States. Since experience tells us that the Federal Reserve will eventually print money to rescue the banking system from deflation caused by credit losses, why didn't Bitcoin surge when AI-related unemployment accelerated? Because the Federal Reserve always needs a crisis to force it to take drastic action. Before the 2008 crisis, the market mantra was "US housing prices will never fall." If they never fell, then the collapse of two little-known Bear Stearns hedge funds in 2007 due to subprime mortgage losses would be insignificant. Similarly, today's mantra is "AI will bring the highest productivity growth in history." To reiterate, if some dubious internet token collapses, or a few shadow banks with terrible risk control suffer the consequences, why should the Federal Reserve intervene? Adding to the Fed's inertia of inaction is its deep-seated political crisis. US President Trump has crossed the Rubicon, launching a criminal investigation into Fed Chairman Powell, the weak, cuckolded, and towel-handing-only figure. Like any institution composed of people, they band together when you threaten the ideological ties that bind them and their so-called lofty mission. Those within the Fed genuinely believe they are independent and above politics, even though history clearly shows that the Fed ultimately always conforms to the president's desired monetary policy. Trump's apparent investigation of Powell for refusing to cut interest rates and print money quickly may provoke Powell's pride, forcing him to remain on the board after his term ends in May. At that time, assuming Trump's new chairman can pass the Senate... (This isn't necessarily true.) Kevin Warsh will face a hostile Federal Reserve. It's said that Warsh supports shrinking the Fed's balance sheet and wants many "radical" reforms to the Fed's operations and bank regulation. Whether other governors think these policies are reasonable is irrelevant; what matters is that Warsh is Trump's man. If Powell remains in office and wins over at least five voting governors, he can defend the Fed's political independence by "doing nothing" to obstruct Trump's agenda. While the Fed is still battling windmills, AI-related unemployment will destroy the balance sheets of American banks. Ultimately, non-TBTF bank stocks will begin to collapse, depositors will flee to the safe haven of JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon, and the credit market will freeze. The market will be frantically asking: Which credit sector will be next to suffer a devastating blow from AI optimization? Because panicked traders only care about immediate liquidity, they will sell everything. The stock market will crash, Bitcoin will trade sideways, or Bitcoin will fall below $60,000 and continue its downward spiral until the Federal Reserve starts printing money. Does the discount window work? Theoretically, if a regulated bank is in trouble and needs to quickly sell assets to meet withdrawals, it can turn to the Federal Reserve's discount window. But the problem is that the discount window assumes the problem is only maturity mismatch and liquidity. However, this time, the loan value on the balance sheet is zero because borrowers simply cannot repay. Once AI takes away your accounting, legal, or investment banking jobs, those jobs will be gone forever. Perhaps after a few years of retraining, you can find work in new AI-driven industries, but that will take many years, and there's no guarantee of success. During this time, if you can't repay your debts, the market will price your car loan debt at zero. This violates the Federal Reserve's collateral standards. To provide banks with unlimited ammunition (printing money) and force them to re-lend to these former "masters of the universe" who are now defaulters, the discount window must be completely overhauled. Unfortunately, this change requires a vote by the governors to approve new policies. Print, baby, print as much as you can! Everyone knows that AI is the most transformative general-purpose technology in human history. The consensus also believes that... Once AI targets arrogant, highly educated lawyers who spend their days piling on paperwork, Lord Elon Musk (or more likely his Chinese rival) will unleash AI robots that will drive labor costs to near zero. Faced with these "truths," the Federal Reserve will have to unleash an unprecedented massive amount of liquidity. Ironically, politicians, terrified of the credit deflation triggered by AI, will gleefully support a new round of "unlimited quantitative easing." There are many ways to print money; I won't guess the specifics. But like the Sunday after Signature Bank collapsed on March 12, 2023, there will be an emergency announcement. "Buffalo Bill" Bessant and the then-Federal Reserve Chairman will announce a joint plan, essentially printing money. Then, "bang," thank goodness, we can back in that damn truck to load up again. The buying frenzy for Bitcoin and altcoins is reminiscent of 2020. Stay flexible. Because AI models are iterating, progress is non-linear. This means that if you thought AI wasn't precise enough for certain knowledge-based tasks last year and assume it won't be now, you're out of touch. This time it's serious; millions of knowledge workers will have to swallow their pride and apply for government assistance like "welfare lazybones" in the slums. I don't think Bitcoin traders could have predicted the Fed's eventual bailout before consumer credit-sensitive stocks were hit hard. I won't short the market, but if you haven't raised cash yet, selling some long positions might be wise. If an asset price falls from 10 to 5, shorting yields a 50% profit; however, if the price rebounds from 5 back to 10, going long can double the return. Therefore, the essence of the game lies in maintaining liquidity, being agile, and specializing in betting on rebounds. Shorting is a game for fools. Always go long on convexity! Once the Fed backs down, Maelstrom will invest its surplus stablecoins in two major altcoins: $ZEC (Zcash) and $HYPE (Hyperliquid). In the next article, I will demonstrate the model to explain why $HYPE rose to $150 in July, about five times higher than its current price. Obviously, we are already long on $ZEC and $HYPE, but I want more. If my AI financial crisis logic holds true, we will have another great opportunity to buy these high-quality altcoins at low prices.
